title: Information Thermodynamics in Generalized Probabilistic Theories
authors: Koki Ono, Shun Umekawa, Hiroyasu Tajima
abstract: Generalized Probabilistic Theories (GPTs) provide a unified framework for describing probabilistic physical theories, encompassing classical and quantum theories as well as hypothetical theories beyond quantum mechanics. Since most GPTs are highly unrealistic and far removed from known physical theories, it is important to constrain them by physically reasonable principles. One of the most important such principles is consistency with thermodynamics, which has been extensively studied through toy models involving semipermeable membranes (SPMs) implementing measurements. On the other hand, information thermodynamics, which plays a central role in understanding the relationship between measurement and thermodynamics in classical and quantum theory, has remained largely undeveloped in GPTs. In this work, we construct information thermodynamics in GPTs and provide a unified framework for analyzing the relationship between measurement, feedback, information erasure, and the second law of thermodynamics. We also formulate a general framework for SPM models and analyze the thermodynamic cost of measurements implemented by SPMs. As a result, we show that no work can be extracted in contradiction with the second law as long as the measurement processes are consistent with entropy nondecrease, and derive sufficient conditions for this property for several entropy definitions proposed in GPTs. Moreover, by considering measurement processes violating these conditions, we construct explicit GPT systems realizing isothermal SPM cycles from which positive work can be extracted. These results demonstrate that violations of the second law can arise from the lack of fundamental entropy properties or discrepancies between entropy definitions, and provide a unified and model-independent foundation for understanding the relationship between thermodynamics and measurement in GPTs.
summary: This paper develops a unified framework for information thermodynamics within Generalized Probabilistic Theories (GPTs). It proves that while a generalized second law holds if certain entropy properties are met, specific non-quantum GPTs can realize cycles that extract work from measurements, highlighting the critical role of entropy definitions in thermodynamic consistency.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of a developed framework for information thermodynamics within Generalized Probabilistic Theories (GPTs) and investigates whether certain GPTs allow for violations of the second law of thermodynamics through measurement-driven processes.
main_result: The authors derive a generalized second law of information thermodynamics for arbitrary GPTs and demonstrate that violations of the second law (positive work extraction from isothermal cycles) can occur in specific GPT models, such as square and regular-hexagon state spaces, due to discrepancies in entropy definitions.
method: The study uses the GPT formalism to construct a unified framework for analyzing measurement, feedback, and erasure, extending the Sagawa-Ueda inequalities and analyzing semipermeable membrane (SPM) models using entropy analysis.
model_or_system: The framework encompasses Generalized Probabilistic Theories (GPTs), including classical and quantum mechanics, as well as hypothetical systems like square and regular-hexagon state spaces. It specifically utilizes semipermeable membrane (SPM) models involving ideal gases with internal GPT degrees of freedom.
key_observables: Extracted work (W_ext), entropy changes (measurement, mixing, and accessible information entropy), and information gain (Groenewold-Ozawa information).
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), measurement efficiency, and entropy properties such as subadditivity and concavity.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes finite-dimensional GPT systems and relies on the existence of concave entropies that are invariant under reversible processes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates generalized information processes; Figures 2-4 depict SPM models for separation, mixing, and general isothermal cycles; Figures 5-6 show explicit SPM cycles in square and hexagon systems that result in positive work extraction.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing a unified framework for information thermodynamics in GPTs, moves to the derivation of generalized Sagawa-Ueda inequalities, analyzes the thermodynamic cost of SPM-based measurements, and concludes by constructing explicit counter-examples (square/hexagon models) where the second law is violated.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum thermodynamics as it provides a rigorous, model-independent foundation for understanding the fundamental relationship between information, measurement, and the second law of thermodynamics beyond the standard quantum mechanical framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
